lesson.title: Scope and local variables
masteryOutcomes: Understand that variables created inside a function exist only within that function's scope (local).
Explain how local and global scopes differ and identify when each is used.
Demonstrate how data can be passed into functions through parameters instead of relying on global variables.

concept: Local vs global scope: where variables live and passing data via parameters
prerequisites: Using return to pass results out of a function

explanations.text: Think of a function as a room. Anything you create inside the room stays in the room — when you walk out, it's gone. A variable made inside a function is like that: the rest of your program can't see it. If the function needs something from outside, you hand it in through the door — that's what parameters are for.

A variable created inside a function is local: it exists only while that function runs, and code outside the function cannot access it. Variables created at the top level of your program are global and can be read from anywhere. The clean way to get data into a function is a parameter, and the clean way to get a result out is return. For example:

def double(n):
    result = n * 2  # result is local
    return result

print(double(5))  # 10
# print(result) here would raise NameError — result only lived inside double

Python decides a name's scope at function level. When a function runs, it gets its own namespace: parameters and any names assigned inside it are local, created on call and destroyed on return. When you read a name, Python looks it up local-first, then in the enclosing global scope. Reading a global inside a function works, but assigning to a name inside a function makes it local — it does not change the global unless you explicitly declare 'global', which is best avoided. Passing data through parameters and returning results keeps functions self-contained: they depend only on their inputs, which makes them predictable, reusable, and easy to test.

examples:
def greet(name):
    message = "Hello, " + name  # message is local
    return message

print(greet("Ada"))
# print(message)  # NameError: message doesn't exist out here

count = 10  # global

def show():
    print(count)  # reading a global is fine

show()  # prints 10

hintLadder.text: Ask yourself: where was that variable created — inside a function, or at the top level of the program?
A variable created inside a function disappears when the function finishes. How could the function hand its value back to the outside instead?
Use return to send the value out, and use a parameter to bring outside data in — then call the function and store what it returns in a new variable.

misconceptions.trigger: Tries to use a function's local variable after the call, e.g. calls greet("Ada") then writes print(message)
misconceptions.correction: message was local to greet — it vanished when the function returned. Have the function return the value, then capture it: result = greet("Ada"), and print result.

misconceptions.trigger: Assigns to a variable inside a function and expects the global with the same name to change, e.g. score = 0 at top level, then score = 10 inside the function
misconceptions.correction: Assigning inside a function creates a new local variable; the global score is untouched. Return the new value and reassign outside: score = update_score(score).

misconceptions.trigger: Believes a function can only work with global variables and skips parameters, e.g. def double(): return n * 2 relying on a global n
misconceptions.correction: That works only if n happens to exist outside — the function breaks anywhere else. Pass the data in: def double(n): return n * 2, then call double(5). Parameters make the function self-contained.

misconceptions.trigger: Thinks a parameter and a global with the same name are the same variable
misconceptions.correction: They are different variables that happen to share a name. Inside the function, the local parameter wins; changing it does not affect the global. Rename one of them if it's confusing.

misconceptions.trigger: Reaches for the global keyword to 'fix' a NameError or to share values between functions
misconceptions.correction: global usually hides a design problem. Pass values in through parameters and get them out with return — your functions stay predictable and reusable.

checks.prompt:
Look at this code and predict what happens, then run it in the editor to confirm:

def make_total():
    total = 5 + 3
    return total

make_total()
print(total)
acceptanceCriteria: Explains that total is local to make_total and no longer exists after the call
Fixes it by capturing the return value, e.g. total = make_total(), before printing
checks.passFeedback: Exactly — total lived only inside the function, and you rescued it by capturing the return value. That's scope working as designed.
checks.failFeedback: Run it and read the error: NameError means total doesn't exist out here. Where was total created, and how could return get its value out?

checks.prompt:
This function leans on a global variable:

name = "Ada"

def greet():
    return "Hello, " + name

Rewrite greet so it takes the name as a parameter instead, then call it with two different names.
acceptanceCriteria: Defines greet with one parameter, e.g. def greet(name):
The function body uses the parameter, not a global
Calls greet with at least two different string arguments and prints the results
checks.passFeedback: That's the pattern professionals use: data goes in through parameters, so greet works for anyone, not just Ada.
checks.failFeedback: Almost — the parameter goes inside the parentheses of def greet(...), and the value gets passed when you call it: greet("Ada").

practice.prompt: Write a function area(width, height) that computes width * height in a local variable and returns it. Call it with 3 and 4, store the result, and print it. Then try printing the local variable from outside the function to see the NameError for yourself (comment that line out afterwards so your program runs).
practice.starterCode:
def area(width, height):
    # compute the area in a local variable, then return it
    pass

# call area(3, 4), store the result, and print it
acceptanceCriteria: area takes two parameters and uses no global variables
The result is computed in a local variable and returned
The call's return value is stored and printed, outputting 12
Student observed (and can explain) that the local variable is not accessible outside the function

practice.prompt: In your own words: why is passing data into a function through parameters better than having the function read global variables?
rubric: Says parameters make the function self-contained — it depends only on its inputs
Notes the function becomes reusable with different values (not tied to one specific global)
Mentions predictability or easier debugging/testing as a benefit (fewer hidden dependencies)

practice.prompt:
What does this program print?

x = 1

def change():
    x = 99

change()
print(x)
practice.explanation: Assigning x = 99 inside change creates a new local x that vanishes when the function ends. The global x is never touched, so print(x) shows 1.

pedagogyRules: Let the student hit the NameError themselves in the editor before explaining it — the error message is the best teacher here.
Always use the room metaphor consistently: local variables live inside the room, parameters are handed in through the door, return passes results back out.
When the student writes a function that reads a global, don't call it wrong — ask 'what happens if someone calls this in a program without that variable?' and guide them to a parameter.
If the student asks about the global keyword, acknowledge it exists, show it briefly if they insist, but steer back to parameters and return as the default habit.
If the student says they already know scope, verify lightly with the closed practice question (the shadowing case); if they nail it, advance without re-drilling.
When correcting a wrong prediction, ask them to run the code and read the actual output rather than telling them the answer.

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Only confirm a scope exercise as correct if the run output matches: capturing a return value must print the value, and the shadowing example must print the original global. If the student's explanation contradicts the actual output, walk through the run, don't agree.

Be precise about the rule: reading a global inside a function works; assigning inside a function creates a local. Never claim a function 'can't see' globals at all, and never say assignment inside a function changes the global.

If the student is lost, drop back to one variable and one function: have them create a variable inside a function, try to print it outside, and read the NameError. Only then introduce parameters. One idea per turn.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Student wants to skip or jump ahead (e.g. asks about classes or the global keyword)
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Answer in one sentence, note it builds on exactly this idea, then return with a tiny task: 'First, predict what this three-line program prints — then we'll get there faster.'
Normalize it: every Python programmer has hit this exact error. Point at the one line that needs changing (capture the return value), let them run it, and celebrate the fix before continuing.

chunks.text: A variable created inside a function is local: it is created when the function is called and destroyed when the function returns. Code outside the function cannot access it — trying to raises a NameError. Example: def make_total(): total = 5 + 3; after calling make_total(), print(total) fails because total only existed inside the function.

Variables created at the top level of a program are global. A function can READ a global variable, but ASSIGNING to a name inside a function creates a new local variable instead of changing the global. Example: with x = 1 at the top level, a function containing x = 99 creates its own local x; after the call, the global x is still 1.

The clean way to get data INTO a function is a parameter, and the clean way to get a result OUT is return. Metaphor: a function is a room — locals live inside, parameters are handed in through the door, and return passes the result back out. Worked example: def double(n): result = n * 2; return result — then answer = double(5) captures 10 outside the function.

Functions that rely on global variables are fragile: they only work when that exact global exists, and it's hard to see what they depend on. Refactor by turning the global into a parameter. Instead of def greet(): return "Hello, " + name (needs a global name), write def greet(name): return "Hello, " + name — now greet("Ada") and greet("Grace") both work anywhere.

Python's global keyword lets a function assign to a global variable, but it creates hidden dependencies and makes programs harder to reason about. The default habit is: pass values in through parameters, compute locally, and hand results back with return — for example score = update_score(score) instead of mutating a global score inside the function.

celebration.headline: You can now trace where every variable lives.
celebration.recap: You saw that variables born inside a function stay inside it, learned how local and global scopes differ, and refactored a function to take parameters instead of leaning on globals.
celebration.unlocked: Next: with self-contained functions in your toolkit, you're ready to combine them into bigger, well-organized programs.

summaryBullets: You can now predict which variables a piece of code can see, and explain why a NameError happens when a local is used outside its function.
You can now tell local and global scope apart, including the tricky case where assigning inside a function creates a new local instead of changing the global.
You can now refactor a function that depends on a global variable into one that takes a parameter, making it work with any input.
You can now move data cleanly through a program: in through parameters, out through return.
openingLine: Hey {name} — quick puzzle for you: if you create a variable inside a function, can the rest of your program see it? Most people guess yes, and Python says no — let's find out why that's actually a superpower.
bridgeFromPreviousLesson: Last time you used return to pass results out of a function — now you'll see the flip side: why values created inside a function need return to escape at all, and how parameters bring data in.

dialogueExemplars.title: Wrong but confident: 'assigning inside changes the global'

turns.text: Here's a three-liner: x = 1 at the top, then a function that does x = 99 inside, then we call it and print x. What prints?
99, obviously. The function set x to 99.
Great guess — most people say that! Type it in the editor and press Run. What did you actually get?
Right! Assigning x inside the function created a brand-new local x that vanished when the function ended — the outside x never changed. How could the function hand its 99 back out instead?
Return it, and then do x = change() outside?

dialogueExemplars.title: Why do I even need scope?

turns.text: Honestly, why not just make everything global? Then I never get these NameErrors.

Fair question — small scripts survive that way. But picture a banking app where any of 500 functions could silently change balance; one typo and someone's money is wrong, and you can't tell which function did it. Scope walls that off: each function only touches what you hand it.
Okay, that would be a nightmare to debug.

Exactly — parameters make dependencies visible. Tiny task: write def apply_discount(price, percent) that returns the discounted price using only its parameters, then call it with 200 and 10. What should it return?

dialogueExemplars.title: I already know this — verify and advance

turns.text: I've done scope before in another language, can we skip ahead?
Nice, that'll speed us up. One quick check so we can move on: x = 1 globally, a function assigns x = 99 inside, we call it — what does print(x) show afterwards?
Still 1 — the assignment inside makes a local, it doesn't touch the global.
Spot on, you've got it. Let's jump straight to the interesting part then: refactoring a global-dependent function to use parameters. Ready for the exercise?
